findings: Destination Port exhibits strong and stable class-conditioned distributional skew across partitions.
DDoS class distribution is dominated by a single port with near-zero entropy, contrasting with the more diverse BENIGN class distribution.
Observed distributional skew is robust despite moderate class imbalance and presence of duplicated rows at partition level.
contradictions: No local contradictions detected; distributional skew aligns with partition-level observations.

findings: Duplicated rows are confirmed at the dataset level and contribute to the structural skew in Destination Port distribution.
Destination Port shows strong distributional divergence between classes with low entropy and dominant values, consistent across verifications.
Entropy variation is bounded and stable despite duplication presence, indicating a robust artifact influence on distributional skew.

findings: Moderate class imbalance (1.577 ratio) exists at partition level with duplicated rows present.
Destination Port shows strong distributional divergence between classes (JS divergence=0.7315).
DDoS class distribution is highly skewed, dominated by a single port with zero entropy.
BENIGN class distribution is more diverse with higher entropy and lower dominant port ratio.
The strong distributional skew in Destination Port contrasts with moderate class imbalance, indicating potential instability or representativeness concerns.
contradictions: Preserved tension between strong distributional skew and moderate class imbalance at partition level.
limitations: Analysis limited to local partition and single feature; no cross-feature or temporal stability checks performed.

findings: Destination Port shows strong dependency concentration but local label neighborhoods exhibit high conflict and duplicate label conflicts.
